results.caption:
They didn't just build ransomware. They built a brand.

DragonForce emerged on BreachForums on December 6, 2023 — not as a lone threat actor, but as a fully structured Ransomware-as-a-Service cartel with a logo, a revenue model, and a recruitment pipeline.

363 confirmed victims. 30+ countries. A 212.5% attack surge.

This is what happens when cybercrime goes corporate.

#DragonForce #Ransomware #RaaS #CyberThreat #CybersecurityAwareness
results.hook: A mythological dragon reborn as ransomware infrastructure creates instant visual dread and brand recognition that stops the scroll.

results.topic: The Binary Signature — Ransom Note Decoded
results.caption:
The note arrives. The clock starts. Your files are gone.

DragonForce's ransom notes aren't just demands — they're a technical signature. ChaCha8 encryption. Cross-platform targeting: Windows, Linux, ESXi. BYOVD drivers killing your EDR before you even know you're hit.

Marks & Spencer. Co-op. Harrods. Summer 2025. £300M+ in combined impact.

The ransom note is just the last thing you see. The damage happened long before.

#DragonForce #RansomNote #ChaCha8 #BYOVD #IncidentResponse
results.hook: A ransom note exploded into its technical components transforms abstract threat intelligence into viscerally readable human impact.

results.caption:
Who is DragonForce? Nobody can definitively say.

Analysts point to Malaysia-based origins — a hacktivist collective that pivoted to ransomware-as-a-service. Others see deliberate obfuscation. No confirmed nation-state nexus. No indictments. No faces.

Just a cartel operating across 30+ countries with 363 victims and a revenue model that lets affiliates keep 80 cents of every dollar extorted.

In cybercrime, the most dangerous actors are the ones nobody can confirm.

#ThreatIntelligence #DragonForce #AttributionGap #RaaS #CyberEspionage
results.hook: Unknown attribution wrapped in geopolitical shadow creates genuine mystery that compels security professionals to engage and debate.

results.caption:
December 6, 2023. 00:00:01 UTC.

A new post appeared on BreachForums. DragonForce was open for business.

Affiliates wanted. Revenue split: 80% yours. 20% ours. Cross-platform encryption ready. EDR evasion built in. Victim portal operational.

Within 18 months: 363 confirmed victims. 30+ countries hit. £300M+ in impact to UK retail alone.

Every catastrophe has a timestamp. This was theirs.

#BreachForums #DarkWeb #DragonForce #RaaS #ThreatIntelligence
results.hook: The exact moment a ransomware cartel goes public on the dark web — rendered as a cinematic event — makes an abstract threat feel terrifyingly real.

results.caption:
March 19, 2025. DragonForce didn't just grow — it reorganized crime itself.

They announced a formal cartel model. Within weeks, RansomHub — one of the most prolific ransomware groups in history — was absorbed into the DragonForce ecosystem on April 8, 2025.

The result: a criminal franchise. Shared infrastructure. White-label ransomware. A marketplace for extortion.

By December 2025, 35 organizations were hit in a single month.

This is what ransomware industrialization looks like.

#DragonForce #RansomHub #RaaSCartel #Ransomware #CybersecurityLeadership
results.hook: The moment a lone ransomware group declares itself a cartel — absorbing competitors and reshaping the criminal ecosystem — is a historic inflection point that demands attention.

results.caption:
DragonForce doesn't hack you. Their franchisees do.

The cartel built a ransomware business with better revenue sharing than most SaaS companies — affiliates keep 80%, the cartel takes 20% off the top.

This isn't a hacker in a hoodie. This is a structured criminal enterprise with recruitment, tooling, support, and profit splits.

The Summer 2025 UK retail attacks — Marks & Spencer, Co-op, Harrods — weren't random. They were affiliate operations running on DragonForce infrastructure.

£300M+ impact. One cartel. Dozens of franchisees.

Know your threat model.

#Ransomware #DragonForce #CyberSecurity #ThreatIntelligence #RansomwareAsAService
results.hook: Visualizing cybercrime as a legitimate franchise model shocks business professionals into sharing because it mirrors their own industry structures.

results.caption:
363 confirmed victims. 30+ countries. A 212.5% surge in attacks.

In December 2025 alone — 35 organizations hit in a single month.

DragonForce didn't scale slowly. They exploded. From BreachForums debut in December 2023 to absorbing RansomHub's entire affiliate network by April 2025 — this cartel grew faster than most legitimate startups.

These aren't statistics. Each number is a breached network, encrypted server, leaked dataset, and a crisis management team working through the night.

The surge is real. The growth curve is steep. The question is whether your defenses scaled to match it.

#DragonForce #Ransomware #CyberThreat #IncidentResponse #CyberSecurity
results.hook: A war-room scoreboard showing real victim counts triggers visceral urgency in security professionals who recognize their own industry sectors on the tally.

results.caption:
No continent was safe. No sector was spared.

DragonForce affiliates operated across 30+ countries — with the US absorbing 52% of confirmed attacks, UK at 12%, and Australia at 6%.

But the remaining 30% spread across Europe, Asia-Pacific, and beyond tells the real story: this is not a regional threat. This is global infrastructure crime.

Retail. Healthcare. Manufacturing. Financial services. Critical national infrastructure.

When a cartel recruits globally, deploys cross-platform encryption (Windows, Linux, ESXi), and splits revenue with dozens of affiliates — geography is not a defense.

Your location is not your protection.

#DragonForce #GlobalCyberThreat #Ransomware #CriticalInfrastructure #CyberResilience
results.hook: A globe being consumed by digital fire with specific country victim counts forces international audiences to see themselves in the data, driving global shares.

results.caption:
Your EDR didn't fail. It was murdered.

DragonForce affiliates use BYOVD — Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver — to terminate EDR and AV processes before dropping ransomware.

Specific weapons: truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys — legitimate, signed Windows kernel drivers repurposed as kill tools.

Because they're signed drivers, they load at kernel level. Above your endpoint security. Above your monitoring. Above your visibility.

By the time ChaCha8 encryption starts, your defenses are already dead.

MITRE ATT&CK T1562.001. Not theoretical. Actively exploited. Right now.

Do you have kernel-level visibility? Can you detect a signed driver killing your security stack?

If you're not sure — that's your answer.

#BYOVD #DragonForce #EDR #Ransomware #ThreatHunting
results.hook: Showing a security tool being physically killed by a trusted driver exploit triggers immediate professional alarm because it visualizes the invisible moment defenders become blind.

results.caption:
No CVE. No exploit kit. No zero-day.

Just a phone call.

DragonForce collaborated with Scattered Spider — the vishing collective responsible for some of the most devastating social engineering attacks in recent memory — to breach organizations through pure human manipulation.

Scattered Spider's playbook: call the IT help desk. Impersonate an employee. Reset the MFA. Get the credentials. Hand the keys to DragonForce affiliates.

The Marks & Spencer breach. Co-op. Harrods. Combined £300M+ impact. Summer 2025.

Your most sophisticated firewall means nothing when your help desk resets an account for anyone who sounds convincing.

Security awareness isn't a checkbox. It's a frontline defense.

#ScatteredSpider #Vishing #SocialEngineering #DragonForce #HumanFirewall
results.hook: Showing a smiling social engineer on a phone call while chaos burns in the background reveals the terrifying simplicity of human hacking, provoking shares from both security and business audiences.

results.caption:
For 9 days, DragonForce operators moved freely through corporate networks — pivoting host to host, harvesting credentials, mapping every server. EDR was blind. The attacker was not.

This is what dwell time looks like from the inside.

MITRE T1021 | T1078 | T1550 — Lateral Movement, Valid Accounts, Pass-the-Hash

The average breach takes 204 days to detect. DragonForce needed only 9 to own everything.

#DragonForce #Ransomware #LateralMovement #CyberSecurity #ThreatIntelligence
results.hook: Nine days of invisible movement through a corporate network is every security team's nightmare made viscerally real.

results.caption:
Before the ransomware even deployed, DragonForce had already taken everything.

6 terabytes. Customer records. Financial data. Intellectual property. Uploaded quietly over days while IT teams watched dashboards that showed nothing unusual.

This is double extortion. Pay for decryption — or watch your data go public.

T1041 — Exfiltration Over C2 | T1567 — Exfiltration to Cloud Storage

Marks & Spencer. Co-op. Harrods. £300M+ impact. Summer 2025.

#DragonForce #DataExfiltration #DoubleExtortion #RansomwareAsAService #CyberDefense
results.hook: Six terabytes of corporate secrets vanishing silently into the dark web is a number that stops every executive scrolling cold.

results.caption:
The ransom note arrives. IT scrambles for the backup server.

It's gone.

DragonForce operators systematically identify and destroy backup systems before deploying encryption. Shadow copies deleted. Tape backup servers wiped. Cloud sync corrupted. They don't just lock your data — they eliminate your escape route.

T1490 — Inhibit System Recovery | T1485 — Data Destruction

'vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet' — four words that end careers.

No backups. No negotiation power. No way out but the ransom.

#DragonForce #BackupDestruction #Ransomware #IncidentResponse #CyberResilience
results.hook: The moment backups are deleted is the moment an organization loses all leverage — and this image makes that devastation visceral and immediate.

results.caption:
Then it hits.

ChaCha8. Cross-platform. Windows. Linux. ESXi hypervisors.

DragonForce's encryption engine doesn't discriminate — it rolls through every system simultaneously. Files renamed. Extensions locked. A ransom note dropped in every directory.

212.5% attack surge year-over-year. 35 organizations in December 2025 alone.

363 victims. 30+ countries. 52% in the United States.

This is what a ransomware cartel at scale looks like when the wave breaks.

#DragonForce #ChaCha8 #RansomwareEncryption #RaaS #CriticalInfrastructure
results.hook: A tidal wave of encryption rolling across a city skyline of servers makes the abstract horror of ransomware deployment emotionally and visually undeniable.

results.caption:
After the encryption. After the backup deletion. After the data exfiltration.

They call you.

DragonForce collaborated with Scattered Spider to conduct vishing campaigns — sophisticated social engineering calls to executives, IT staff, and help desks. Human voices. Fake authority. Pressure tactics.

And if you don't pay? Your 6TB goes public. Your customers get notified. Your share price collapses.

80% of ransom revenue goes to affiliates. 20% to the cartel. This is an industry.

DragonForce BreachForums debut: December 6, 2023. Cartel announced: March 19, 2025.

#DragonForce #ScatteredSpider #Vishing #SocialEngineering #RansomwareCartel
results.hook: A phone ringing in a devastated executive office — the moment the cartel calls to negotiate — is the most personally terrifying frame in any ransomware story.

results.caption:
On April 8, 2025, DragonForce didn't just grow — it absorbed an entire rival cartel.

RansomHub's affiliate network, infrastructure, and victim pipeline folded into DragonForce overnight. One announcement on a dark web forum and the threat landscape shifted permanently.

This is what consolidation looks like in the ransomware economy. Fewer cartels. More power. Less friction for attackers.

The business model is maturing. Is your defense program keeping pace?

#Ransomware #DragonForce #RansomHub #ThreatIntelligence #CyberSecurity
results.hook: A corporate hostile takeover visualized as a criminal empire merger instantly communicates scale and danger to security leaders who've watched the threat intelligence reports roll in.

results.caption:
Marks & Spencer. Co-op. Harrods.

Three of Britain's most recognized retail brands. One summer. One ransomware cartel.

DragonForce's collaboration with Scattered Spider turned vishing calls into a £300M+ catastrophe that rippled across the entire UK retail sector. Online orders suspended. Loyalty systems offline. Internal communications compromised.

This wasn't sophisticated zero-day exploitation. It started with a phone call.

The most expensive vulnerabilities are the ones sitting in your helpdesk.

#DragonForce #UKCyberSecurity #Ransomware #RetailSecurity #ScatteredSpider
results.hook: Three household British brand names on a single ransomware damage report creates immediate recognition and visceral impact for anyone who shops on the UK high street.

results.topic: Full MITRE Kill Chain — All 10 Steps
results.caption:
Here is exactly how DragonForce gets from a vishing call to full encryption.

10 steps. Documented. Repeatable. Devastating.

T1566 → T1078 → T1059 → T1484 → T1562 → T1055 → T1486 → T1490 → T1567 → T1657

The BYOVD technique using truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys to kill your EDR tools is not theoretical. It is operational. It has been used against 363 confirmed victims across 30+ countries.

Print this. Put it on your wall. Know every step before they take it.

#MITRE #ATTACKFramework #DragonForce #ThreatHunting #BlueTeam
results.hook: A single image mapping DragonForce's actual MITRE ATT&CK kill chain gives security practitioners a shareable reference they will save, screenshot, and share with their entire team.

results.caption:
DragonForce has 363 confirmed victims. Here is what separated the organizations that survived from the ones that paid.

5 defenses. All of them operationally validated against this exact kill chain.

① Phishing-resistant MFA — kills the vishing vector at step 1
② BYOVD driver blocklisting — truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys are known. Block them.
③ Privileged access workstations — breaks lateral movement at step 6
④ Immutable offline backups — neutralizes T1490 backup deletion
⑤ Helpdesk identity verification protocols — the £300M breach started with a phone call

The dragon has a known attack path. Build walls at every step.

#CyberDefense #ZeroTrust #DragonForce #IncidentResponse #SecurityArchitecture
results.hook: Actionable defense frameworks in visual form get saved and reshared by practitioners who need to justify security investments to leadership — this becomes a reference asset, not just a post.

results.topic: The Dragon Is Already Inside The Walls
results.caption:
By the time you see the ransom note, the dragon has been inside your walls for 14 hours.

T+0: Vishing call to helpdesk.
T+2: Credentials harvested. Valid account. No alerts.
T+6: Lateral movement. Living off the land. No alerts.
T+14: truesight.sys loaded. Your EDR: dead.
T+18: ChaCha8 encryption begins. 363 organizations learned this timeline too late.

DragonForce is not breaking through your walls. They are walking through your front door with your own employee's badge.

The perimeter is not the problem. Identity is the perimeter now.

212.5% attack surge. 35 organizations in December 2025 alone.

The dragon is patient. Are you prepared?

#DragonForce #Ransomware #IdentitySecurity #ZeroTrust #CyberSecurity
results.hook: A wide cinematic landscape of a burning enterprise network with a dragon coiled inside the perimeter delivers the visceral realization that perimeter defense alone is catastrophically insufficient — designed for maximum emotional impact and sharing.
